titleStmt.title: A Scattering Matrix Formalism to Model Periodic Heat Diffusion in Stratified Solid Media

funder.orgName: National Natural Science Foundation of China

affiliation.orgName: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Design & Manufacture or Micro/Nano Biomedical Instruments
Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Design & Manufacture or Micro/Nano Biomedical Instruments

analytic.title: A Scattering Matrix Formalism to Model Periodic Heat Diffusion in Stratified Solid Media

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: The transfer matrix formalism is widely used in modeling heat diffusion in layered structures. Due to an intrinsic numerical instability issue, which has not yet drawn enough attention to the heat transfer community, this formalism fails at high heating frequencies and/or in thick structures. Inspired by its success in modeling wave propagation, we develop a numerically-stable scattering matrix framework to model periodic heat diffusion in stratified solid media. As a concreate example, we apply this scattering matrix methodology to the 3 рқң” method. We first validate our framework using various well-known solutions. Next, we demonstrate the numerical stability of the framework using a configuration that resembles the three-dimensional stacked architecture for chip packing. Last, we propose synthetic "experiments" to exhibit, under certain circumstances, the merits of the scattering matrix formalism in extracting thermal properties.

div.head: I. Introduction
div.p: The transfer matrix method has been widely applied to model periodic heating problems in layered structures, since Carslaw and Jaeger developed the analogous heat transfer formalism [1] to its electric [2] and elastic [3][4] counterparts. Feldman replaced the commonly used temperature and flux vector components with counter-propagating thermal waves [5] , making it more analogous to the treatment in electromagnetices and optics. This matrix methodology has subsequently been implemented to analyze experimental data of well-known techniques for thermal measurements at the nanoscale, such as the 3рқң” method [6][7][8][9] , the time-domain thermoreflectance (TDTR) method [10][11][12][13] and the frequency-domain thermoreflectance (FDTR) method [14][15] .

However, this transfer matrix formalism has an intrinsic numerical instability problem, which has not yet drawn enough attention to the heat transfer community. This problem arises from the fact that one diagonal element of the matrix exponentially decays while the other diagonal element exponentially blows up as the film thickness and/or the heating frequency increases. This issue has long been ignored since it only manifests itself when the heating frequency and/or the total thickness of the multilayer stack increases to some critical value that was of no practical interest. For example, for a SiO2/Cu (50nm/50nm) alternating multilayer stack, the transfer matrix approach starts to break down only when the heating frequency increases to beyond GHz for microns thick stack (see, for example, Fig. 3c). This GHz heating frequency was thought to be unpractical before, but is becoming reasonable in the context of the evergrowing clock frequency of CPUs.

On the other hand, the wave community had long been suffered from this numerical instability problem [16][17][18][19][20][21] . Until 1988, did Ko and Inkson address this shortcoming by introducing the so-called scattering matrix scheme to model electron transmission in nonperiodic semiconductor heterostructures [22] . The essential idea is to regroup the inputs and outputs bridged by the matrix such that both of its diagonal elements exponentially decay, and thus avoid the numerical problem of the transfer matrix methodology. In 1999, Whittaker and Culshaw introduced this scattering matrix scheme to model the propagation of electromagnetic waves through multilayer structures [23] . However, to our knowledge, this numerically stable scattering matrix methodology has not yet been employed to treat the underlying heat transfer model of any experimental techniques.

Here we present the scattering matrix formalism to model periodic heat diffusion in stratified solid media. In Section II, we highlight the numerical instability problem of the transfer matrix scheme, and introduce the scattering matrix formalism using the one-dimensional diffusion equation. We consider the most general scenario in which the heater line is buried inside the multilayer structure. In Section III, we generalize the scattering matrix framework to multidimensions, with the 3 рқң” method as a concrete example. As sanity checks, we verify the framework using widely known solutions. In Section IV, we demonstrate the numerical stability of the scattering matrix formalism using a configuration that mimics the three-dimensional integration technology for processor architecture design.

In Section V, we demonstrate with synthetic "experiments" that, in certain scenarios, this scattering matrix formalism could be employed to extract thermal properties, which is challenging for other schemes.

div.head: II. Mathematical framework
div.p: In this section, we review the widely used transfer matrix method to solve heat diffusion problems [6][7][8][9][10] , and point out its numerical instability issue. To address this shortcoming, we introduce the concept of the scattering matrix used in the electrical and the optical communities [22][23] , and apply it to model heat diffusion in layered structures.

div.head: A. Transfer matrix formalism and its numerical instability issue
div.p: We start with the one-dimensional (1D) heat diffusion equation,
div.formula: 1 рқҗ· рқң•рқ‘Ү рқң•рқ‘Ў = рқң• 2 рқ‘Ү рқң•рқ‘§ 2 ,(1)
div.p: where рқҗ·(вүЎ рқ‘ҳ рқҗ¶ вҒ„ ) is the thermal diffusivity, рқ‘ҳ is thermal conductivity, and рқҗ¶ is the volumetric specific heat.

According to the Linear Response Theorem [1] , the temperature response of a linear system under a periodic heating flux (рқ‘һ в„Һрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ рқ‘’ рқ‘–рқң” рқҗ» рқ‘Ў ) excitation reads as О”рқ‘Ү(рқ‘§, рқ‘Ў) = рқңғ(рқ‘§) рқ‘’ рқ‘–рқң” рқҗ» рқ‘Ў , (2) where О”рқ‘Ү вүЎ рқ‘Ү -рқ‘Ү рқ‘ҹрқ‘’рқ‘“. , and рқ‘Ү рқ‘ҹрқ‘’рқ‘“. is a reference temperature, e.g. the ambient temperature, рқ‘Ү вҲһ . рқң” рқҗ» = 2рқңӢрқ‘“ рқҗ» = 4рқңӢрқ‘“ is the angular heating frequency, and рқ‘“ is the frequency of the driving current.
Substituting Eq. 2 into Eq. 1, one obtains
div.formula: рқ‘–рқң” рқҗ» рқҗ· рқңғ = рқң• 2 рқңғ рқң•рқ‘§ 2 . (3
div.p: The general solution of Eq. 3 in layer рқ‘— of a multilayer structure (Fig. 1a) is
div.formula: рқңғ рқ‘— (рқ‘§) = рқ‘Һ рқ‘— | рқ‘§=рқ‘§ рқ‘— рқ‘’ -рқ‘ў рқ‘— (рқ‘§-рқ‘§ рқ‘— ) + рқ‘Ҹ рқ‘— | рқ‘§=рқ‘§ рқ‘— рқ‘’ рқ‘ў рқ‘— (рқ‘§-рқ‘§ рқ‘— ) ,(4)

div.formula: рқ‘ў рқ‘— = вҲҡ рқ‘–рқң” рқҗ» рқҗ· рқ‘— ,(5)
div.p: is the inverse of the thermal penetration depth [24] in layer рқ‘—. Applying Fourier's law to Eq. 4, one obtains the corresponding heat flux, which, combining with Eq. 4, leads to [5] [
div.formula: рқ‘Һ рқ‘—+1 | рқ‘§=рқ‘§ рқ‘—+1 рқ‘Ҹ рқ‘—+1 | рқ‘§=рқ‘§ рқ‘—+1 ] = рқҗ“ рқҗЈ,рқҗЈ+рқҹҸ Г— [ рқ‘Һ рқ‘— | рқ‘§=рқ‘§ рқ‘— рқ‘Ҹ рқ‘— | рқ‘§=рқ‘§ рқ‘— ],(6)
div.p: where the transfer matrix, рқҗ“ рқҗЈ,рқҗЈ+рқҹҸ = рқҗ„ рқҗЈ,рқҗЈ+рқҹҸ Г— рқҡҪ рқҗЈ , bridges the temperature and heat flux between layers j and j +1 (Fig. 1a), in which
div.formula: рқҗ„ рқҗЈ,рқҗЈ+рқҹҸ = 1 2рқ‘’ рқ‘—+1 [ рқ‘’ рқ‘—+1 + рқ‘’ рқ‘— рқ‘’ рқ‘—+1 -рқ‘’ рқ‘— рқ‘’ рқ‘—+1 -рқ‘’ рқ‘— рқ‘’ рқ‘—+1 + рқ‘’ рқ‘— ](7)
div.p: is the effusivity matrix, and
div.formula: рқҡҪ рқҗЈ = [ рқ‘’ -рқ‘ў рқ‘— рқ‘‘ рқ‘— 0 0 рқ‘’ рқ‘ў рқ‘— рқ‘‘ рқ‘— ](8)
div.p: is the phase matrix. Here рқ‘’ рқ‘— = вҲҡ рқ‘ҳ рқ‘— рқҗ¶ рқ‘— is the effusivity, and рқ‘‘ рқ‘— is the thickness of layer j.

The phase matrix (Eq. 8) contains both exponentially decaying and growing entries, which leads to numerical instability issues when the heating frequency and/or the total thickness of the stratified medium increases to some critical values (see, for example, open circles in Fig. 3).

div.head: B. Scattering matrix formalism 1. Basic idea
div.p: Motivated by the treatment of the propagation of electromagnetic waves in thick and lossy systems [23] , here we develop a scattering matrix scheme to analyze periodic heating problems in multilayer structures. First, we rewrite Eq. 4 as
div.formula: рқңғ рқ‘— (рқ‘§) = рқ‘Һ рқ‘— | рқ‘§=рқ‘§ рқ‘— рқ‘’ -рқ‘ў рқ‘— (рқ‘§-рқ‘§ рқ‘— ) + рқ‘Ҹ рқ‘— | рқ‘§=рқ‘§ рқ‘— +рқ‘‘ рқ‘— рқ‘’ -рқ‘ў рқ‘— (рқ‘§ рқ‘— +рқ‘‘ рқ‘— -рқ‘§) ,(9)
div.p: which chooses a different reference point, рқ‘§ = рқ‘§ рқ‘— + рқ‘‘ рқ‘— , for рқ‘Ҹ рқ‘— (see Fig. 1b). As a result, the second term turns to exponentially decaying. Second, we skew the input and output of Eq. 6 to arrive at
div.formula: [ рқ‘Һ рқ‘—+1 | рқ‘§=рқ‘§ рқ‘— +рқ‘‘ рқ‘— рқ‘Ҹ рқ‘— | рқ‘§=рқ‘§ рқ‘— +рқ‘‘ рқ‘— ] = рқҗ’ рқҗЈ,рқҗЈ+рқҹҸ Г— [ рқ‘Һ рқ‘— | рқ‘§=рқ‘§ рқ‘— рқ‘Ҹ рқ‘—+1 | рқ‘§=рқ‘§ рқ‘—+1 +рқ‘‘ рқ‘—+1 ],(10)
div.p: where рқҗ’ рқҗЈ,рқҗЈ+рқҹҸ is the so-called scattering matrix [22][23] . These two steps above ensure numerical stability of the scattering matrix formalism (see, for example, solid lines in Fig. 3).

div.head: Recursive relations to obtain рқҗ’ рқҗў,рқҗЈ+рқҹҸ from рқҗ’ рқҗў,рқҗЈ
div.p: Analogous to the scattering matrix formalism in electromagnetics [23] , we now develop the recursive relations to obtain рқҗ’ рқҹҸ,рқҗҚ from рқҗ’ рқҹҸ,рқҹҸ in heat transfer problems.

By definition, рқҗ’ рқҹҸ,рқҹҸ is an identity matrix. Therefore, the key here is to obtain рқҗ’ рқҗў,рқҗЈ+рқҹҸ from рқҗ’ рқҗў,рқҗЈ . First, for any layer i that is above layer j (Fig. 2), one applies Eq. 10 to obtain
div.formula: [ рқ‘Һ рқ‘— рқ‘Ҹ рқ‘– ] = рқҗ’ рқҗў,рқҗЈ Г— [ рқ‘Һ рқ‘– рқ‘Ҹ рқ‘— ],(11)
div.p: in which, for brevity, we neglect the reference points for the coefficients, рқ‘Һ рқ‘– , рқ‘Ҹ рқ‘– рқ‘Һ рқ‘— , and рқ‘Ҹ рқ‘— . Second, combining the temperature (Eq. 9) and its corresponding flux in layer j, one obtains
div.formula: [ рқңғ рқ‘— (рқ‘§) рқ‘һ рқ‘— (рқ‘§) ] = рқҗҢ рқҗЈ Г— [ рқ‘Һ рқ‘— рқ‘’ -рқ‘ў рқ‘— (рқ‘§-рқ‘§ рқ‘— ) рқ‘Ҹ рқ‘— рқ‘’ -рқ‘ў рқ‘— (рқ‘§ рқ‘— +рқ‘‘ рқ‘— -рқ‘§) ],(12)
div.p: where
div.formula: рқҗҢ рқҗЈ = [ 1 1 рқӣҫ рқ‘— -рқӣҫ рқ‘— ],(13)
div.p: and рқӣҫ рқ‘— = рқ‘ҳ рқ‘—,рқ‘§ рқ‘ў рқ‘— (14) Third, applying Eq. 12 to layer j+1, i.e., replacing the index j with j+1, and assuming continuity condition for both the temperature and flux at the interface between layers j and j+1, one obtains
div.formula: [ рқ‘Һ рқ‘— рқ‘’ -рқ‘ў рқ‘— рқ‘‘ рқ‘— рқ‘Ҹ рқ‘— ] = рқҗҲ рқҗЈ,рқҗЈ+рқҹҸ Г— [ рқ‘Һ рқ‘—+1 рқ‘Ҹ рқ‘—+1 рқ‘’ -рқ‘ў рқ‘—+1 рқ‘‘ рқ‘—+1 ],(15)
div.p: where рқҗҲ рқҗЈ,рқҗЈ+рқҹҸ = рқҗҢ рқҗЈ -рқҹҸ Г— рқҗҢ рқҗЈ+рқҹҸ (16) is the interface matrix. To incorporate the thermal contact resistance, рқ‘… рқ‘җ,рқ‘—-рқ‘—+1 " (with SI units of [m 2 KW -1 ]), between layers рқ‘— and рқ‘— + 1, one updates Eq. 16 to
div.formula: рқҗҲ рқҗЈ+рқҹҸ,рқҗЈ вҖІ = рқҗҢ рқҗЈ -рқҹҸ Г— рқҗҢ рқҗЈ+рқҹҸ вҖІ ,(17)
div.p: where
div.formula: рқҗҢ рқҗЈ+рқҹҸ вҖІ = [ 1 + рқ‘… рқ‘җ,рқ‘—-рқ‘—+1 " рқӣҫ рқ‘—+1 1 -рқ‘… рқ‘җ,рқ‘—-рқ‘—+1 " рқӣҫ рқ‘—+1 рқӣҫ рқ‘—+1 -рқӣҫ рқ‘—+1 ].(18)
div.p: At last, combining Eqs. 11 and 15, one develops the recursive relations to obtain рқҗ’ рқҗў,рқҗЈ+рқҹҸ from рқҗ’ рқҗў,рқҗЈ , as derived in Appendix B and summarized in Table I.

div.head: The driving periodic heating flux and boundary conditions
div.p: We consider a general case, in which the driving flux (рқ‘һ в„Һрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ рқ‘’ рқ‘–рқң” рқҗ» рқ‘Ў ) is exerted at the interface between layers рқ‘— and рқ‘— + 1 by a heater with negligible thickness (Fig. 2). In this case, Eq. 15 is updated to
div.formula: рқҗҢ рқҗЈ+рқҹҸ Г— [ рқ‘Һ рқ‘—+1 рқ‘Ҹ рқ‘—+1 рқ‘’ -рқ‘ў рқ‘—+1 рқ‘‘ рқ‘—+1 ] -рқҗҢ рқҗЈ Г— [ рқ‘Һ рқ‘— рқ‘’ -рқ‘ў рқ‘— рқ‘‘ рқ‘— рқ‘Ҹ рқ‘— ] = рқ‘һ в„Һрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ [ 0 1 ].(19)
div.p: We discuss two types of commonly used boundary conditions, an adiabatic boundary condition and an isothermal boundary condition. In the latter we assume the top and bottom boundaries are fixed at the same рқ‘Ү рқ‘ҹрқ‘’рқ‘“. , which results in рқңғ рқ‘Ҹрқ‘‘рқ‘Ұ = 0.
For the top boundary, one has
div.formula: рқ‘Һ 1 -рқ‘җ рқ‘Ҹрқ‘‘рқ‘Ұ рқ‘Ҹ 1 рқ‘’ -рқ‘ў 1 рқ‘‘ 1 = 0.(20)
div.p: Likewise, for the bottom boundary one arrives at
div.formula: рқ‘җ рқ‘Ҹрқ‘‘рқ‘Ұ рқ‘Һ рқ‘Ғ рқ‘’ -рқ‘ў рқ‘Ғ рқ‘‘ рқ‘Ғ -рқ‘Ҹ рқ‘Ғ = 0.(21)
div.p: where рқ‘җ рқ‘Ҹрқ‘‘рқ‘Ұ = 1 or -1 for an adiabatic or isothermal boundary condition.
If the top or bottom layer is semi-infinite, Eq. 20 or 21 reduces to
div.formula: рқ‘Һ 1 = 0 or рқ‘Ҹ рқ‘Ғ = 0,(22)
div.p: which is equivalent to set рқ‘җ рқ‘Ҹрқ‘‘рқ‘Ұ = 0.
We summarize different values of рқ‘җ рқ‘Ҹрқ‘‘рқ‘Ұ according to different boundary conditions,
div.formula: рқ‘җ рқ‘Ҹрқ‘‘рқ‘Ұ = { 1, adiabatic -1, isothermal 0, semi -infinite . (23

div.head: Recipe: solving the temperature field
div.p: Using the recursive relations (Table I), the driving flux (Eq. 19), and the boundary conditions (Eqs. 20-23), one can solve the temperature profile of the multilayer structure, following the recipe below.
First, using Eq. 11, one obtains
div.formula: [ рқ‘Һ рқ‘— рқ‘Ҹ 1 ] = рқҗ’ рқҹҸ,рқҗЈ Г— [ рқ‘Һ 1 рқ‘Ҹ рқ‘— ],(24)
div.p: and
div.formula: [ рқ‘Һ рқ‘Ғ рқ‘Ҹ рқ‘—+1 ] = рқҗ’ рқҗЈ+рқҹҸ,рқҗҚ Г— [ рқ‘Һ рқ‘—+1 рқ‘Ҹ рқ‘Ғ ],(25)
div.p: where рқҗ’ рқҹҸ,рқҗЈ and рқҗ’ рқҗЈ+рқҹҸ,рқҗҚ can be obtained using the recursion relations in Table I. 24 or 25 by replacing the index j or j+1 to other indices of interest. Knowing these coefficients, one obtains the temperature field of the multilayer structure (Eq. 9).
div.formula: Next

div.head: III. Apply the framework to the 3 рққҺ method
div.p: In section II, we develop the scatter matrix formalism for the one-dimensional (1D) diffusion equation. We now generalize the framework to two-dimensional (2D) in this section, using the 3 рқң” method as a concrete example.

div.head: A. From 1D to 2D
div.p: Although the original 3рқң” method was derived in a cylindrical coordinate [1,24] , here, to take into account the anisotropy of films and substrates, we follow Ref. [25] and choose a Cartesian coordinate. For simplicity, we assume the principal axes of the thermal conductivity tensor are aligned with the natural cartesian coordinate system defined by the heater line and sample surface, i.e., the thermal conductivity tensor is diagonalized. We briefly comment on the more general case of an anisotropic thermal conductivity tensor with finite off-diagonal terms in Appendix C.
Isolating the time dependence and performing the Fourier cosine transform (along the x-axis, see Fig. 1) to the 2D diffusion equation, one obtains
div.formula: рқ‘–рқң” рқҗ» рқҗ· рқ‘§ рқңғ Мғ= -рқ‘ӣ рқ‘Ҙрқ‘§ рқңҶ 2 рқңғ Мғ+ рқң• 2 рқңғ вҲӮрқ‘§ 2 , (30
div.p: where рқ‘ӣ рқ‘Ҙрқ‘§ = рқ‘ҳ рқ‘Ҙ рқ‘ҳ рқ‘§ вҒ„ is the anisotropy ratio, and рқ‘ҳ рқ‘Ҙ and рқ‘ҳ рқ‘§ are the in-plane and the cross-plane thermal conductivities. As compared to Eqs. 3 and 5, Eq. 30 is now an ordinary differential equation with an updated complex wave vector [7] рқ‘ў Мғрқ‘— = вҲҡ рқ‘ӣ рқ‘—,рқ‘Ҙрқ‘§ рқңҶ 2
div.formula: + рқ‘–рқң” рқҗ» рқҗ· рқ‘—,рқ‘§ ,(31)
div.p: where
div.formula: рқҗ· рқ‘—,рқ‘§ = рқ‘ҳ рқ‘—,рқ‘§ рқҗ¶ рқ‘—
div.p: is the thermal diffusivity of layer j along z-axis.

For the general case where the heater is located at the interface between layers j and j+1 (Fig. 2), one can still apply Eq. 9 to obtain the spatial component of the temperature of the heater in Fourier space:
div.formula: рқңғ Мғв„Һрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ = рқңғ Мғрқ‘—+1 | рқ‘§=рқ‘§ рқ‘—+1 = рқ‘Һ рқ‘—+1 + рқ‘Ҹ рқ‘—+1 рқ‘’ -рқ‘ў Мғрқ‘—+1 рқ‘‘ рқ‘—+1 = (рқ‘Һ рқ‘—+1 вҖІ + рқ‘Ҹ рқ‘—+1 вҖІ рқ‘’ -рқ‘ў Мғрқ‘—+1 рқ‘‘ рқ‘—+1 )рқ‘һ Мғв„Һрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ , (32
div.p: where the normalized coefficients, рқ‘Һ рқ‘—+1 вҖІ = рқ‘Һ рқ‘—+1 /рқ‘һ Мғв„Һрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ and рқ‘Ҹ рқ‘—+1 вҖІ = рқ‘Ҹ рқ‘—+1 /рқ‘һ Мғв„Һрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ , are computed using Table II. As compared to the 1D scenario, here the only difference is to replace рқ‘ў (Eq. 5) with рқ‘ў Мғ (Eq. 31), and to replace рқ‘һ в„Һрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ (Table II) with its Fourier cosine transform, рқ‘һ Мғв„Һрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ , which will be illustrated with examples in Section III-C. Transforming Eq. 32 back to the real space, one obtains the spatial component of the temperature of the heater:
div.formula: рқңғ в„Һрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ (рқ‘Ҙ) = 2 рқңӢ вҲ« рқңғ Мғв„Һрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ вҲһ 0 cos(рқңҶрқ‘Ҙ) рқ‘‘рқңҶ.(33)

div.head: B. Most common scenario: line heater on sample
div.p: Section II-B gives a recipe to implement the scattering matrix formalism, in which the heater is embedded inside the sample (Fig. 2). To apply this general framework to a special and also most common scenario, line heater on sample, here we highlight some key steps to illustrate how to use Tables I-II.

First, treat the air/vacuum above the sample as a semi-infinite layer [5] , and denote it as layer 0. Since the effusivity рқ‘’ рқ‘Һрқ‘–рқ‘ҹ вүӘ рқ‘’ рқ‘ рқ‘ңрқ‘ҷрқ‘–рқ‘‘ , we neglect the heat conduction through layer 0, and thus using Eq. 13 we obtain
div.formula: рқҗҢ рқҹҺ = [ 1 1 0 0 ].(34)
div.p: Next, replace, in Eq. 28 and Eq. 29, both the subscripts 1 and j with 0 in matrices рқҗ’ рқҹҸ,рқҗЈ and рқҗ’ рқҗЈ+рқҹҸ,рқҗҚ , the complex wave vector рқ‘ў рқ‘— (or, рқ‘ў Мғрқ‘— in the 2D scenario here), and the layer thickness рқ‘‘ рқ‘— . Therefore one obtains рқӣј = 0 (Eq. 28), regardless of the boundary condition. On the other hand, рқӣҪ (Eq. 29) depends on the boundary condition and рқҗ’ рқҹҸ,рқҗҚ that can be obtained from the recursion relations in Table I. At last, solving for рқ‘Һ 1 and рқ‘Ҹ 1 using Table II, and substituting them into Eqs. 32 and 33, one obtains
div.formula: рқңғ в„Һрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ-рқ‘ңрқ‘ӣ-рқ‘Ўрқ‘ңрқ‘қ (рқ‘Ҙ) = 2 рқңӢ вҲ« 1+рқӣҪрқ‘’ -рқ‘ў Мғ1рқ‘‘ 1 рқӣҫ Мғ1(1-рқӣҪрқ‘’ -рқ‘ў Мғ1рқ‘‘ 1 ) рқ‘һ Мғв„Һрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ cos(рқңҶрқ‘Ҙ) вҲһ 0 рқ‘‘рқңҶ,(35)
div.p: where рқӣҫ Мғ1 = рқ‘ҳ 1 рқ‘ў Мғ1 is defined in Eq. 14.

div.head: C. Verification of the framework
div.p: We verify the framework using widely known solutions, as discussed in detail in the following and summarized in Table III. Note here we treat all the substrates to be semi-infinite.
We consider two types of heaters. First, if the line heater is infinitely-narrow, one has
div.formula: рқ‘һ в„Һрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ = рқ‘ғ 0 рқ‘ҷ рқӣҝ(рқ‘Ҙ),(36)

Equation 46 can be regrouped to рқңғ в„Һрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ-рқ‘ңрқ‘ӣ-рқ‘Ўрқ‘ңрқ‘қ,рқ‘Һрқ‘Јрқ‘”. = рқңғ рқ‘ рқ‘ўрқ‘Ҹ. + рқңғ рқ‘“рқ‘–рқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡ , (48) where рқңғ рқ‘ рқ‘ўрқ‘Ҹ. is computed using Eq. 42, and рқңғ рқ‘“рқ‘–рқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡ = рқңғ рқ‘“рқ‘–рқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡ,1рқҗ· Г— рқ‘ , (49) where the dimensionless scaling factor, рқ‘ (see Appendix E), indicates how much deviation the 1D approximation,

With further approximations (see Appendix E), and assuming isotropic properties of the sample, one reduces Eq. 48 to the well-known series-resistor solution Note here as compared to Eqs. 1 and 2 of Ref. [27], Eq. 51 includes the effect of the thermal contact resistance, рқ‘… рқ‘җ,рқ‘“рқ‘–рқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡ-рқ‘ рқ‘ўрқ‘Ҹ. " .

div.head: Line heater sandwiched by two film-substrate stacks (3 rd row of Table III)
div.p: This configuration has to be solved using the general framework (Eqs. 32 and 33, instead of Eq. 35). Here we again consider a line heater with finite width, рқ‘Ө. Using Tables I-II, we compute the S-matrices, and correspondingly рқӣј and рқӣҪ , and finally

(рқңҶ рқ‘Ө 2 ) 2 вҲһ 0 рқ‘‘рқңҶ. (52
div.p: where рқҗө 21 Вұ and рқҗө 34 Вұ are evaluated using Eq. 47 with updated subscripts. Note here 1, 2, 3, and 4 denote the upper substrate, the upper film, the lower film, and the lower substrate, respectively (see the schematic in Table III). After some re-arrangements, Eq. 52 recovers the solution using the transfer matrix formalism [8][9] . Note here for simplicity we ignore the contact thermal resistance between layers. If the upper substrate (layer 1) and film (layer 2) are the same materials, i.e. рқ‘ҳ 1 = рқ‘ҳ 2 = рқ‘ҳ рқ‘ўрқ‘қрқ‘қрқ‘’рқ‘ҹ and рқҗ· 1 = рқҗ· 2 = рқҗ· рқ‘ўрқ‘қрқ‘қрқ‘’рқ‘ҹ , and so are the lower film (layer 3) and substrate (layer 4), i.e. (53) For the special case of рқ‘ӣ рқ‘ўрқ‘қрқ‘қрқ‘’рқ‘ҹ,рқ‘Ҙрқ‘§ рқҗ· рқ‘ўрқ‘қрқ‘қрқ‘’рқ‘ҹ,рқ‘§ = рқ‘ӣ рқ‘ҷрқ‘ңрқ‘Өрқ‘’рқ‘ҹ,рқ‘Ҙрқ‘§ рқҗ· рқ‘ҷрқ‘ңрқ‘Өрқ‘’рқ‘ҹ,рқ‘§ = рқ‘ӣ рқ‘Ҙрқ‘§ рқҗ· рқ‘§ , also known as the boundary mismatch approximation (BMA) [28] , one simplifies Eq. 53 to
div.formula: рқңғ в„Һрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ-рқ‘ рқ‘Һрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘рқ‘Өрқ‘–рқ‘җв„Һрқ‘’рқ‘‘,рқ‘Һрқ‘Јрқ‘”. = рқ‘ғ 0 рқңӢрқ‘ҷ( вҲҡ рқ‘ҳ рқ‘ўрқ‘қрқ‘қрқ‘’рқ‘ҹ,рқ‘Ҙ рқ‘ҳ рқ‘ўрқ‘қрқ‘қрқ‘’рқ‘ҹ,рқ‘§ +вҲҡрқ‘ҳ рқ‘ҷрқ‘ңрқ‘Өрқ‘’рқ‘ҹ,рқ‘Ҙ рқ‘ҳ рқ‘ҷрқ‘ңрқ‘Өрқ‘’рқ‘ҹ,рқ‘§ ) вҲ« 1 вҲҡрқңҶ 2 + рқ‘–рқң” рқҗ» рқ‘ӣ рқ‘Ҙрқ‘§ рқҗ· рқ‘§ рқ‘ рқ‘–рқ‘ӣ 2 (рқңҶ рқ‘Ө 2 ) (рқңҶ рқ‘Ө 2 ) 2 вҲһ 0 рқ‘‘рқңҶ.
div.p: (54) If one further assumes isotropic properties, Eq. 54 reduces to
div.formula: рқңғ в„Һрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ-рқ‘ рқ‘Һрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘рқ‘Өрқ‘–рқ‘җв„Һрқ‘’рқ‘‘,рқ‘Һрқ‘Јрқ‘”. = рқ‘ғ 0 рқңӢрқ‘ҷ(рқ‘ҳ рқ‘ўрқ‘қрқ‘қрқ‘’рқ‘ҹ +рқ‘ҳ рқ‘ҷрқ‘ңрқ‘Өрқ‘’рқ‘ҹ ) вҲ« 1 вҲҡрқңҶ 2 + рқ‘–рқң” рқҗ» рқҗ· рқ‘§ рқ‘ рқ‘–рқ‘ӣ 2 (рқңҶ рқ‘Ө 2 ) (рқңҶ рқ‘Ө 2 ) 2 вҲһ 0 рқ‘‘рқңҶ,(55)
div.p: which recovers Eq. B2 of Ref. [28].

div.head: IV. Demonstration of the numerical stability of the framework
div.p: To demonstrate the numerical stability of the scattering matrix scheme, here we consider a 20 рқңҮрқ‘ҡ -wide line heater sandwiched by two identical superlattice-onsubstrate structures (Fig. 3a). We choose a 500 рқңҮрқ‘ҡ -thick silicon substrate, and 50nm/50nm-thick SiO2/Cu alternating films as the building blocks of the superlattice, with corresponding thermal conductivities [29] of рқ‘ҳ рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– =148 W/m-K, рқ‘ҳ рқ‘Ҷрқ‘–рқ‘Ӯ2 =1.38 W/m-K, and рқ‘ҳ рқҗ¶рқ‘ў = 401 W/m-K, and the thermal contact resistance [30] of рқ‘… рқ‘җ,рқ‘Ҷрқ‘–рқ‘Ӯ 2 -рқҗ¶рқ‘ў " = 1 Г— 10 -8 m 2 /W-K. Note here we neglect the possible size effect on рқ‘ҳ рқҗ¶рқ‘ў for the nanoscale Cu film. This configuration mimics the three-dimensional stacked architecture for chip packing [31][32] .

Figure 3b shows the temperature rise of the heater normalized by the corresponding periodic heating power, i.e. the thermal resistance, рқ‘… (= рқңғ в„Һрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ,рқ‘Һрқ‘Јрқ‘”. /рқ‘ғ 0 ), as a function of the number of periods (рқ‘Ғ = рқ‘Ғ 1 + рқ‘Ғ 2 ) at a fixed heating frequency, рқ‘“ рқҗ» = 10 Hz, calculated using both the transfer matrix scheme (open circles) and the scattering matrix scheme (solid line). While both approaches work well and agree with each other for configurations with small рқ‘Ғ, the transfer matrix scheme breaks down at a critical total number of periods, рқ‘Ғ рқ‘җ = 44. In contrast, the scattering matrix scheme works reliably regardless of рқ‘Ғ , as expected. We note that although the exact value of рқ‘Ғ рқ‘җ may depend on the specific numerical method to implement the integration (Eq. 33) and/or the specification of the computer, the transfer matrix approach is destined to fail at some point when рқ‘Ғ keeps increasing, as discussed in Section II-A.

In addition to the failure for thick structures, the transfer matrix scheme is also destined to break down at high heating frequencies. This is confirmed by Fig. 3c, in which we fix рқ‘Ғ 1 = рқ‘Ғ 2 = 20 (and thus рқ‘Ғ = 40 ) and plot the рқ‘… vs. рқ‘“ рқҗ» relation. While the transfer matrix approach (open circles) fails beyond a critical heating frequency, рқ‘“ рқҗ»,рқ‘җ вүҲ 3GHz, the scattering matrix approach (solid line) is robust over the entire frequency range of interest. Like рқ‘Ғ рқ‘җ in Fig. 3b, this рқ‘“ рқҗ»,рқ‘җ is destined to appear somewhere, although its exact value may depend on the specific integration method and/or the specification of the computer. Generalizing the results of Figs. 3b-c leads to the regime map in Fig. 3d. In the lower regime (white), both the transfer matrix approach and the scattering matrix approach work well in computing the temperature response of the heater, while in the other regime (gray shaded), the transfer matrix formalism breaks down but the scattering matrix formalism is still robust.

We briefly comment on the periodic heating in the GHz regime and beyond. First, this high frequency heating is becoming realistic in integrated circuits in the context that the clock frequency of CPU has already been in the same order of magnitude. Second, the thermal wave in this regime is well localized inside the top layer for рқ‘“ рқҗ» > рқ‘“ рқҗ»,рқ‘җ . For example, the critical penetration depth, рқҗҝ рқ‘қрқ‘’рқ‘ӣрқ‘’рқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ.,рқ‘җ = вҲҡрқҗ· рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– /2рқңӢрқ‘“ рқҗ»,рқ‘җ вүҲ 6.6nm (Eq. 44), which is much smaller than the thickness of the upper and lower SiO2 layers adjacent to the heater, рқ‘‘ рқ‘Ҷрқ‘–рқ‘Ӯ 2 =50 nm. Correspondingly, an approximate solution is to treat the SiO2 layers as semi-infinite substrates and compute the temperature rise of the heater using Eq. 43. Third, although the ballistic phonon transport should be considered in this regime [33][34][35][36] . it is still the paradigm of experimental techniques in which solutions of the diffusion equation are used to fit for effective thermal properties from experimental data [37][38][39] .

div.head: V. Synthetic "experiments" to extract thermal properties
div.p: In addition to the application to the complicated multilayer structure in Fig. 3, the scattering matrix formalism can manifest its merits in experimentally extracting thermal properties even for the simple film-on-substrate configuration under certain circumstances. To demonstrate these merits, we generate three sets of synthetic "experimental" data points using a 3D finite element model (FEM) [40] , including Вұ1% of "experimental noise" (open circles in Fig. 4). In the 3D FEM model, we fix the thickness of the silicon substrate to be рқ‘‘ рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– = 500рқңҮрқ‘ҡ, and set the width and the length (into the page) of the heater to be рқ‘Ө = 20рқңҮрқ‘ҡ and рқ‘ҷ = 2,000рқңҮрқ‘ҡ, respectively. We exert a driving periodic heat flux on the top surface of the heater, and an isothermal boundary condition (рқ‘Ү вҲһ ) on the bottom surface of the silicon substrate, with all the other surfaces insulated. The input thermal properties to the FEM model are summarized in Table IV. We verify that the size of the film and the substrate is large enough to ensure numerical convergence. We choose a heating frequency range of 500-2,000 Hz to ensure the assumption of a narrow heater on a semi-infinite substrate (Appendix F).

For all three scenarios (rows 1-3 of Fig. 4), the thermal conductivity of the silicon substrate, рқ‘ҳ рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– , is obtained by the slope method [24] , in which the slope of the linear relation of рқ‘… рқ‘ рқ‘Ұрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ўв„Һ.-рқ‘’рқ‘Ҙрқ‘қрқ‘Ў. vs. ln (рқ‘“ рқҗ» ) gives рқ‘ҳ рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– . Here рқ‘… рқ‘ рқ‘Ұрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ўв„Һ.-рқ‘’рқ‘Ҙрқ‘қрқ‘Ў. = рқңғ в„Һрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ,рқ‘Һрқ‘Јрқ‘”. /рқ‘ғ 0 , and рқңғ в„Һрқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ,рқ‘Һрқ‘Јрқ‘”. is the amplitude of the temperature oscillation averaged across the width of the heater line. To extract рқ‘ҳ рқ‘“рқ‘–рқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡ , we apply three approaches. In the first approach (1 st column of Fig. 4), we compute the thermal resistance of the substrate, рқ‘… рқ‘Ҷрқ‘–-рқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘‘рқ‘’рқ‘ҷ , using Eq. 45 and рқ‘ҳ рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– from the slope method. Then we obtain the thermal resistance of the thin film, рқ‘… рқ‘“рқ‘–рқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡ = рқ‘… рқ‘ рқ‘Ұрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ўв„Һ,.-рқ‘’рқ‘Ҙрқ‘қрқ‘Ў. -рқ‘… рқ‘Ҷрқ‘–-рқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘‘рқ‘’рқ‘ҷ , and correspondingly рқ‘ҳ рқ‘“рқ‘–рқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡ assuming 1D heat conduction across the thin film [27,41] .

Note in this 1D approximation approach, рқ‘… рқ‘“рқ‘–рқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡ and thus рқ‘ҳ рқ‘“рқ‘–рқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡ include the effect of the thermal contact resistance between the film and the substrate, рқ‘… рқ‘җ,рқ‘Ҷрқ‘–рқ‘Ӯ 2 -рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– " . In the second (2 nd column) or the third (3 rd column) approach, we treat рқ‘ҳ рқ‘“рқ‘–рқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡ as the only free parameter to fit Eq. 46 (or Eq. 35) to the synthetic "experiments" using the transfer matrix (or the scattering matrix) formalism, respectively.

The first scenario (upper row of Fig. 4) shows a sanity check using a standard configuration of the classic 3рқң” measurement, a 0.3рқңҮрқ‘ҡ-thick low-рқ‘ҳ SiO2 thin film on a high-рқ‘ҳ silicon substrate. In this scenario, all the three methods succeed in extracting рқ‘ҳ рқ‘Ҷрқ‘–рқ‘Ӯ 2 . Note in this scenario we include a thermal contact resistance, рқ‘… рқ‘җ,рқ‘Ҷрқ‘–рқ‘Ӯ 2 -рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– " = 1 Г— 10 -8 m 2 -K/W [27] , in the synthetic "experiment" (i.e. the FEM model; Table IV), but neglect it in the fitting scheme using either the transfer matrix or the scattering matrix formalism, since the intrinsic рқ‘ҳ SiO 2 dominates in this scenario.

The second scenario (middle row of Fig. 4) replaces the SiO2 film with a 4 рқңҮрқ‘ҡthick high-рқ‘ҳ diamond film, which closely resemble a proposed configuration of post-silicon heat sinks [42][43] . It is very challenging to measure this high-рқ‘ҳ-film-on-substrate configuration, because the 3рқң” method gradually loses its sensitivity to рқ‘ҳ рқ‘“рқ‘–рқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡ as it surpasses рқ‘ҳ рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– (Appendix G). In addition, the anisotropic nature of рқ‘ҳ рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘ and the exist of рқ‘… рқ‘җ,рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘-рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– " that cannot be neglected in this scenario because of the high рқ‘ҳ рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘ make the measurement even more complicated. In the following, we will show that the scattering matrix framework offers a way to extract the cross-plane thermal conductivity of the diamond film, рқ‘ҳ рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘,рқ‘§ , for this high-рқ‘ҳ -film-onsubstrate configuration, regardless of the thickness of the diamond film.

As can be seen from Fig. 4d, the 1D approximation approach fails in measuring this high-рқ‘ҳ -film-on-substrate configuration, because рқ‘… рқ‘ рқ‘Ұрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ўв„Һ,.-рқ‘’рқ‘Ҙрқ‘қрқ‘Ў. < рқ‘… рқ‘Ҷрқ‘–-рқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘‘рқ‘’рқ‘ҷ , which implies a negative рқ‘… рқ‘“рқ‘–рқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡ We now come to a dilemma: even if we neglect рқ‘… рқ‘җ,рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘-рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– " , how could the effective resistance, рқ‘… рқ‘ рқ‘Ұрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ўв„Һ,.-рқ‘’рқ‘Ҙрқ‘қрқ‘Ў. = рқ‘… рқ‘Ҷрқ‘–-рқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘‘рқ‘’рқ‘ҷ + рқ‘… рқ‘“рқ‘–рқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡ , be smaller than рқ‘… рқ‘Ҷрқ‘–-рқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘‘рқ‘’рқ‘ҷ

We qualitatively resolve this seemingly paradoxical problem using the following two-step argument. First, replace the SiO2 film in the first scenario (1 st row of Fig. 4) with a silicon film. If one neglects the size effect of рқ‘ҳ рқ‘Ҷрқ‘–-рқ‘“рқ‘–рқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡ (i.e. we assign рқ‘ҳ рқ‘Ҷрқ‘–-рқ‘“рқ‘–рқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡ = рқ‘ҳ рқ‘Ҷрқ‘–-рқ‘ рқ‘ўрқ‘Ҹрқ‘ рқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹрқ‘Һрқ‘Ўрқ‘’ here), it is not hard to conclude that рқ‘… рқ‘ рқ‘Ұрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ўв„Һ,.-рқ‘’рқ‘Ҙрқ‘қрқ‘Ў. = рқ‘… рқ‘Ҷрқ‘–-рқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘‘рқ‘’рқ‘ҷ in the heating frequency range that ensures рқҗҝ рқ‘қрқ‘’рқ‘ӣрқ‘’рқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ. вүӘ рқ‘‘ рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– , because the combination of a silicon thin film and a semi-infinite silicon substrate is no more than a single semi-infinite silicon substrate. Second, replace the silicon film with a diamond film. Now it is easy to conclude that рқ‘… рқ‘ рқ‘Ұрқ‘ӣрқ‘Ўв„Һ,.-рқ‘’рқ‘Ҙрқ‘қрқ‘Ў. < рқ‘… рқ‘Ҷрқ‘–-рқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘‘рқ‘’рқ‘ҷ , since рқ‘ҳ рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘,рқ‘§ > рқ‘ҳ рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– .
Therefore, in this high-рқ‘ҳ -film-on-substrate scenario, the 1D approximation approach fails. We will return to this interpretation quantitatively in the context of Fig. 5 shortly.

We now apply the transfer matrix and the scattering matrix methods to fit for рқ‘ҳ рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘,рқ‘§ . Since рқ‘ҳ рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– and рқҗ¶ рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– had been accurately measured and well documented, the sensitivity analysis in Appendix G indicates that, among the remaining input parameters, рқ‘ҳ рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘,рқ‘§ is most sensitive to рқ‘… рқ‘җ,рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘-рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– " and рқ‘ҳ рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘,рқ‘Ҙ . We obtain рқ‘… рқ‘җ,рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘-рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– " by applying the 1D approximation to a 0.1рқңҮрқ‘ҡ -thick diamond film on silicon substrate configuration, in which рқ‘… рқ‘“рқ‘–рқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡ is dominated by рқ‘… рқ‘җ,рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘-рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– " (see Appendix G for more details). We further assume рқ‘ҳ рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘,рқ‘Ҙ is obtained using the suspended microfabricated-device method with typical measurement uncertainty of 10% [44] . Fitting either the transfer matrix or the scattering matrix model to one set of typical synthetic "experiment", one obtains the only fitting parameter, рқ‘ҳ рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘,рқ‘§ , with 9.4% error as compared to the input value (Figs. 4e and4f; Table IV).

The last scenario (lower row of Fig. 4) increases the thickness of the diamond film, рқ‘‘ рқ‘“рқ‘–рқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡ , from 4рқңҮрқ‘ҡ to 7.5рқңҮрқ‘ҡ. In this case, the "transfer matrix" approach also fails due to its intrinsic numerical instability, as discussed in Section II-A. On the other hand, the scattering matrix approach is still robust in fitting рқ‘ҳ рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘,рқ‘§ with an error of 2.7% for one set of typical synthetic "experiment" (Figs. 4i; Table IV). In Appendix G, we conduct multiple synthetic "experiments" to analyze the confidence interval of these "measurements".

At last, we turn to quantifying our qualitative interpretation to the dilemma in Figs. 4d and4g. Recall Eq. 49, the full expression of the temperature rise of the film, рқңғ рқ‘“рқ‘–рқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡ . Figure 5a shows a contour plot of the dimensionless scaling factor рқ‘ (Eq. E1 of Appendix E), which indicates how much deviation the 1D approximation, рқңғ рқ‘“рқ‘–рқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡ,1рқҗ· , is away from the true рқңғ рқ‘“рқ‘–рқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡ . The x-axis is the contrast factor between the thermal conductivity of the film and the substrate, рқӣұ 1 = рқ‘ҳ рқ‘“рқ‘–рқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡ,рқ‘§ /рқ‘ҳ рқ‘ рқ‘ўрқ‘Ҹ.,рқ‘§ , and the y-axis is the spreading factor of the film, рқӣұ according to the dimensions of the heater and the sample (Fig. 4) and the thermal properties summarized in Table IV.

The fact that рқӣұ 3 вүӘ 1 , рқӣұ 5 < 1 , and рқҗҝ рқ‘қрқ‘’рқ‘ӣрқ‘’рқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ.,рқ‘ рқ‘ўрқ‘Ҹ. вүӘ рқ‘‘ рқ‘ рқ‘ўрқ‘Ҹ. may suggest that 1D approximation is justified. However, Fig. 5a shows that the series-resistor solution (Eq. 51) could still introduce significant errors when the contrast factor (рқӣұ 1 ) and/or the spreading factor of the film (рқӣұ 4 ) are relatively large. This result confirms the negative рқ‘… рқ‘“рқ‘–рқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡ observed in Figs. 4d, which is indicated using a triangle in Fig. 5a. Likewise, Fig. 5b shows a contour plot of рқ‘ with the y-axis replaced by the dimensionless thermal contact resistance, рқӣұ 6 = рқ‘… рқ‘җ,рқ‘“рқ‘–рқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡ,рқ‘ рқ‘ўрқ‘Ҹ. " рқ‘ҳ рқ‘“рқ‘–рқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡ /рқ‘‘ рқ‘“рқ‘–рқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡ . With рқӣұ 2 = 0.021 , рқӣұ 3 = 0.045 , рқӣұ 4 = 0.34 , and рқӣұ 5 = 0.75, this contour plot confirms the negative рқ‘… рқ‘“рқ‘–рқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡ observed in Figs. 4g, which is indicated using a circle in Fig. 5b.

div.head: VI. Conclusions and Discussion
div.p: To circumvent the intrinsic numerical instability problem of the transfer matrix formalism, we have generalized the scattering matrix methodology of wave propagation to model periodic heat diffusion in layered structures. We applied this scattering matrix framework to the 3 рқң” method, and validated it with various wellknown solutions. Then we demonstrated its numerical stability in modeling the periodic heating of a thick 3D stacked architecture for chip packing at high heating frequency. We also proposed synthetic "experiments" to show that this framework can be used to extract thermal properties in certain circumstances when the conventional techniques break down.

We conclude by commenting on the potential application of this scattering matrix framework to two other popular experimental techniques, the time-domain thermoreflectance (TDTR) [10][11][12][13] and the frequency-domain thermoreflectance (FDTR) [14][15] . As compared to the experimental setup of the 3 рқң” method, TDTR and FDTR can be pushed to much higher heating frequencies, typically at tens of to hundreds of MHz [37,39] . Moreover, TDTR and FDTR rely more on the transfer matrix formalism to fit for thermal properties of multilayer structures [10][11][12][13][14][15]39] . These facts make the scattering matrix formalism an ideal choice for these laser techniques to avoid numerical instability issues of the transfer matrix methodology. To summarize the analogy and the contrast, we briefly outline the key steps of implementing the scattering matrix framework to the 1D planar heating, the 2D 3 рқң” method, and the 3D TDTR/FDTR in Table V. For the 3D case (last column of Table V), the Fourier cosine transform is replaced with a Hankel transform, and the complex wave vector, рқ‘ў Мғ , is updated correspondingly [10][11][12][13][14][15] .

figure.head: Fig. 1 .Fig. 2 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 1. Comparison between (a) transfer matrix and (b) scattering matrix formalism.The former uses two coefficients, рқ‘Һ рқ‘— and рқ‘Ҹ рқ‘— , at the same reference point, рқ‘§ = рқ‘§ рқ‘— , to represent the temperature field (see Eq. 4 of the main text) and the corresponding heat flux in layer j. The temperature field and flux in layer j+1 are linked to those in layer j using the transfer matrix. Although straightforward, this transfer matrix formalism is numerically unstable, as discussed in session II-A of the main text. To circumvent this problem, the scattering matrix formalism in this work changes the reference point from рқ‘§ = рқ‘§ рқ‘— to рқ‘§ = рқ‘§ рқ‘—+1 , and skews the input and output of the transfer matrix formalism.

(d) Regime map: While the transfer matrix framework fails at a critical рқ‘Ғ рқ‘җ or рқ‘“ рқҗ»,рқ‘җ , the scattering matrix methodology is robust everywhere in the рқ‘“ рқҗ» -N plane.

To extract the crossplane thermal conductivity of the film, рқ‘ҳ рқ‘“рқ‘–рқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡ,рқ‘§ , three methods (columns) are applied to three scenarios (rows). рқ‘ҳ рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– is obtained using the slope method[24] for all three sets of "experiments." While the 1D approximation (1 st column) method and the transfer matrix methodology (2 nd column) successively fail (Figs.4d, 4g and 4h), the scattering matrix formalism (last column) remains reliable. See Appendix G for more details on the sensitivity and uncertainty analyses.

div.head: Acknowledgements
div.p: This work was supported in part by National Natural Science Foundation of China (51776038).

APPENDIX B: Derivation of the recursive relations in Table I of the main text APPENDIX C: The general case of an anisotropic thermal conductivity tensor with finite off-diagonal elements APPENDIX D: More details on how to implement the scattering matrix method APPENDIX E: Dimensionless scaling factor that evaluates the 1D approximation APPENDIX F: Quantifying the error of Eq. 45 APPENDIX G: Sensitivity and uncertainty analysis (1,2)рқҗҲ рқҗЈ,рқҗЈ+рқҹҸ (2,1) APPENDIX F: Quantifying the error of Eq. 45

The widely used heat transfer model (Eq. 45) of the 3рқң” method assumes semiinfinite substrate heated by a narrow heater. This assumption requires (1) рқҗҝ рқ‘қрқ‘’рқ‘ӣрқ‘’рқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ. вүӘ рқ‘‘ рқ‘ рқ‘ўрқ‘Ҹ. and (2) рқҗҝ рқ‘қрқ‘’рқ‘ӣрқ‘’рқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ. вү« рқ‘Ө/2, where рқҗҝ рқ‘қрқ‘’рқ‘ӣрқ‘’рқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ. = вҲҡрқҗ· рқ‘ рқ‘ўрқ‘Ҹ. /2рқңӢрқ‘“ рқҗ» (Eq. 44).
These requirements set the proper frequency range of the measurements.

An open question here is how big of the substrate is big enough Likewise, how narrow of the heater line is narrow enough A common practice is to give an empirical safe factor, e.g. рқҗҝ рқ‘қрқ‘’рқ‘ӣрқ‘’рқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ. вүӨ 1 5 рқ‘‘ рқ‘ рқ‘ўрқ‘Ҹ. and рқҗҝ рқ‘қрқ‘’рқ‘ӣрқ‘’рқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ. вүҘ 5 Г— (рқ‘Ө/2) [45] . Dames bridges these safe factors to their corresponding error tolerance [46] .
To answer the open question above systematically, we justify the applicable regime of Eq. 45 using the general solution, Eq. 35.

The latter is not constrained by the two requirements above. Here we define a relative error, рқ‘’рқ‘ҹрқ‘ҹ. = (рқ‘… -рқ‘… рқ‘ рқ‘’рқ‘ҡрқ‘–-рқ‘–рқ‘ӣрқ‘“. )/ рқ‘… рқ‘ рқ‘’рқ‘ҡрқ‘–-рқ‘–рқ‘ӣрқ‘“. , where рқ‘… рқ‘ рқ‘’рқ‘ҡрқ‘–-рқ‘–рқ‘ӣрқ‘“. is the thermal resistance defined by Eq. 44, and рқ‘… is the thermal resistance (Eq. 35) corresponding to the isothermal (solid line) and the adiabatic (dashed line) boundary conditions, respectively. For example, Fig. F1a reads that рқ‘‘ рқ‘Ҷрқ‘ўрқ‘Ҹ. /рқҗҝ рқ‘қрқ‘’рқ‘ӣрқ‘’рқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ. > 2.40 ensures рқ‘’рқ‘ҹрқ‘ҹ. < Вұ5% . Likewise, Fig. F1b suggests that рқҗҝ рқ‘қрқ‘’рқ‘ӣрқ‘’рқ‘Ўрқ‘ҹ. /(рқ‘Ө/2) > 1.35 ensures рқ‘’рқ‘ҹрқ‘ҹ. < Вұ5%. We note here in Fig. E1b the far-side boundary condtion does not matter any more, and thus we show only the result of the isothermal boundary condition.

In practical measurements in a cryostat where a specific temperature point is set and mornitored using a thermocouple anchored near the chip carrier mounted on the copper "cold finger", one might think an isothermal boundary condition for the bottom surface of the sample describes the reality most properly. However, this isothermal boundary condition requires an infinite large thermal conductivity of the "cold finger" and the ceramic chip carrier. This requirement cannot be satisfied, even though the thermal conductivity of copper is relatively high. On the other hand, one may choose to suspend the sample and correspondingly use an adiabatic boundary condition. However, in this case, it is not convenient to conduct measurements under various temperature points. Therefore, the semi-infinite boundary condition is still the most practical one, as long as we ensure requirement (1) above is satisfied according to Fig. E1a.

div.head: APPENDIX G: Sensitivity and uncertainty analysis
div.p: First, we conduct the sensitivity analysis to determine the important input parameters to the transfer matrix or the scattering matrix model. We define the sensitivity of the output fitting parameter, рқ‘ҳ рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘,рқ‘§ , to an input parameter of interest, рқ‘қ, as [47] S рқ‘қ рқ‘ҳ рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘,рқ‘§ = | рқң•(ln рқ‘ҳ рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘,рқ‘§ ) рқң•(ln рқ‘қ)

div.head: |. (G1)
div.p: For example, S рқ‘қ рқ‘ҳ рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘,рқ‘§ = 2 means a 10% change in рқ‘қ would cause a 20% change in the "measured" рқ‘ҳ рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘,рқ‘§ . Two details are worth noting here. First, the 10% change in рқ‘қ applies only to the transfer matrix or the scattering matrix model, but not to the FEM model. Second, the Вұ1% of "experimental noise" is not included in this sensitivity analysis. Among the input thermal properties summarized in Table IV of the main text, Fig. G1 indicates that рқ‘ҳ рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘,рқ‘§ is most sensitive to the substrate properties, рқ‘ҳ рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– and рқҗ¶ рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– , due to the fact that рқ‘ҳ рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘ > рқ‘ҳ рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– and рқ‘‘ рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘ вүӘ рқ‘‘ рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– . While in the application of microprocessors, doped silicon is selected to facilitate the electrical manipulation, here in the measurement of the thermal property of the diamond film, we have the freedom to use the undoped silicon substrate that has well documented values of рқ‘ҳ рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– and рқҗ¶ рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– [29] . Therefore, we focus on the second most important group of input parameters, рқ‘ҳ рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘,рқ‘Ҙ and рқ‘… рқ‘җ,рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘-рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– " . Figure G1 рқ‘ҳ рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘,рқ‘Ҙ can be measured separately using other techniques, e.g. the suspended microfabricated-device method with typical measurement uncertainty of 10% [44] , as mentioned in the main text and outlined in Table IV.

To obtain рқ‘… рқ‘җ,рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘-рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– " , we generate synthetic "experimental data" from a 0.1рқңҮрқ‘ҡ -thick-diamond-film-on-Si-substrate configuration, and again invoke the 1D approximation. But this time, instead of рқ‘ҳ рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘,рқ‘§ , we extract рқ‘… рқ‘җ,рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘-рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– " , since in this configuration, рқ‘… рқ‘җ,рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘-рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– " dominates рқ‘… рқ‘“рқ‘–рқ‘ҷрқ‘ҡ,рқ‘’рқ‘“рқ‘“. , as evident from Eq. G2.

With Вұ1% "experimental noise", the "measured" рқ‘… рқ‘җ,рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘-рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– " agrees with the input one to the FEM model to within 17%, as outlined in Table IV. Fig. G1. Sensitivity of the "measured" рқ‘ҳ рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘,рқ‘§ to various input thermal properties in the thickness range of the diamond film from 1 to 10 Ојm. While the scattering matrix model is valid in the full thickness range of interest, the transfer matrix (TM) model fails for thicker films, as indicated by the gray-shaded area. рқ‘ҳ рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘,рқ‘§ is most sensitive to рқ‘ҳ рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– and рқҗ¶ рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– , but fortunately these two properties have been well studied and documented. Therefore, to reduce the uncertainty of the рқ‘ҳ рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘,рқ‘§ "measurements" using the trasnfer matrix and the scattering matrix methods (Fig. 4), we focus on obtaining the second most important set of input parameters to the transfer matrix and the scattering matrix model: рқ‘ҳ рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘,рқ‘Ҙ and рқ‘… рқ‘җ,рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘-рқ‘Ҷрқ‘– " (see text for details.) Next, we conduct the uncertainty analysis. Figure 4 of the main text shows one set of synthetic "experimental data" (symbols) generated using the FEM model with Вұ1% "experimental noise" for each of the three configurations, and fits these data to either the transfer matrix (2 nd column) or the scattering matrix model (3 rd column) with some of the input parameters deviated from those to the FEM model, as outlined in Table IV and justified above. The only free parameter, рқ‘ҳ рқ‘Ҷрқ‘–рқ‘Ӯ 2 (1 st row) or рқ‘ҳ рқҗ·рқ‘–рқ‘Һрқ‘ҡрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘‘,рқ‘§ (2 nd and 3 rd rows), is obtained and shown in the corresponding entry of Fig. 4.

To mimic the real measurements, in which one usually conducts the experiment several times to obtain the average value and the standard deviation to represent the "true" result and the measurement uncertainty, we generate many sets of such synthetic "experiments" to obtain the statistics. For example, Fig. G2 shows the results of 100 trials of synthetic "experiments" for the scenario of the 7.5рқңҮрқ‘ҡ-thick diamond film (3 rd row of Fig. 4), which gives the 95% confidence interval of the vitual "experiments," 679 to 745 W/m-K.

analytic.title: Transmission of Elastic Waves through a Stratified Solid Medium
